network.overall_strategy: This network is designed as a literature-faithful scenario map rather than a claim of solved prebiotic arginine synthesis. It centers the best-supported constructive route: surface cyanosulfidic protometabolism leading from HCN/acetylene to acrylonitrile, beta-aminopropionitrile, beta-aminopropionaldehyde, cyanamide capture, and an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. Parallel lower-confidence but literature-discussed branches include direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide, a hypothetical cyanate/citrulline prebiotic urea-cycle analog, and a trace supercritical CO2/H2O route reported by Fujioka et al. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively to supply simple reduced carbon feedstocks such as formate and acetate and to frame cross-environment provisioning, while avoiding unsupported claims of robust hydrothermal arginine synthesis because arginine is thermolabile. Downstream biochemical steps emphasize montmorillonite-mediated concentration/protection and wet-dry peptide incorporation once arginine is formed.

reactions.name: Greigite-assisted hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 to one-carbon reduced species on iron-sulfur phases.
reactions.reasoning: Included as hydrothermal feedstock provisioning consistent with iron-sulfur world carbon fixation and greigite/mackinawite catalysis; hydrothermal amino acid synthesis is thermodynamically favorable near vent interfaces but not specifically demonstrated for arginine. This step is used conservatively to connect hydrothermal reduced-carbon supply to surface chemistry (Kitadai, 2015; Jordan et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Magnetite-catalyzed hydrothermal conversion of formate to acetate

conditions.other: anoxic vent setting; Fischer-Tropsch-type/reductive carbon fixation regime

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction and C-C bond formation among one-carbon species on iron oxide/metal surfaces produces small carboxylates.
reactions.reasoning: Used as a plausible hydrothermal reduced-carbon provisioning step rather than a direct arginine-forming step, in line with vent carbon-fixation frameworks. This avoids overstating hydrothermal arginine synthesis while maintaining required hydrothermal network integration (Kitadai, 2015; Amend & Shock, 1998).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal generation/provision of pyruvate from reduced carbon pool

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor or vent-mixing setting; network-connecting step

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated carbon fixation and homologation yield alpha-keto acid intermediates such as pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: This is a cautious network-bridging step based on general hydrothermal alpha-keto acid plausibility rather than an arginine-specific experimental demonstration. It is included to support the literature-reported pyruvate-based trace route and broader prebiotic carbon economy without claiming robust hydrothermal arginine production (Kitadai, 2015; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Cu-photoredox cyanosulfidic cross-coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile
agents: sulfide minerals (molybdenite/stibnite analog for cyanosulfidic sulfur handling)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system with phosphate buffer
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV-irradiated cyanosulfidic conditions with H2S as reductant

reactions.mechanism: Copper photoredox chemistry couples cyanide-derived carbon with acetylene to form the three-carbon nitrile backbone.
reactions.reasoning: This is the opening step of the best-supported prebiotic arginine-precursor route. Conditions follow the cyanosulfidic protometabolism framework: HCN, H2S, Cu(I)/Cu(II), phosphate buffer, UV light, anaerobic, about 35C (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Ammonia addition to acrylonitrile giving beta-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration on clay under cyanosulfidic conditions
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV-driven network context with H2S/Cu photoredox in the same geochemical setting

reactions.mechanism: Conjugate addition of ammonia to activated nitrile alkene produces the beta-amino nitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This follows the Patel/Sutherland arginine-precursor sequence in which acrylonitrile reacts with NH3 to form beta-aminopropionitrile under cyanosulfidic protometabolism conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox in cyanosulfidic sulfur/copper environment
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV-irradiated; reduction step embedded in Patel/Sutherland network

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction of nitrile functionality furnishes the corresponding beta-amino aldehyde precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report explicitly places beta-aminopropionitrile reduction before guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde. Exact isolated yield details were not reported, but the sequence and network context are from the cyanosulfidic protometabolism study and follow-up discussion (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photochemical formation/provision of cyanamide in surface reduced-gas chemistry

conditions.other: reduced atmospheric or shallow-pool photochemical setting; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reduced nitrogen/carbon chemistry generates cyanamide as a condensed nitrile-nitrogen species.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is strongly supported as prebiotically available and central to arginine proposals, but no single universally accepted stoichiometric route is established in the provided literature. This node is therefore framed as photochemical formation/provision under reduced-gas UV chemistry rather than an overcommitted detailed mechanism (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015; Powner et al., 2009).

reactions.name: UV conversion of ammonium cyanide chemistry to guanidine

conditions.catalyst: 254 nm UV with iron-associated catalytic context
conditions.other: pH 8.4-8.5, 7 days to 10 weeks, ammonium cyanide solution, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oligomerization/rearrangement of cyanide/nitrogen chemistry yields guanidine.
reactions.reasoning: Guanidine formation in 8-20% yield from UV-irradiated ammonium cyanide solutions is experimentally documented and provides a literature-grounded auxiliary guanidino pool, though not a direct arginine synthesis route (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Efficient guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide to arginine aminonitrile-class precursor

conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV-irradiated; guanylation especially efficient according to source

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine attack on cyanamide installs a guanidino equivalent, followed by further photoreduction/HCN incorporation to the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction compresses the literature-described sequence: beta-aminopropionaldehyde reacts especially efficiently with cyanamide, and further photoreduction plus HCN addition furnishes compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine. Compression is necessary for graph economy, but the chemistry and conditions are taken directly from the Patel/Tsanakopoulou route (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pool or interface; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile into the corresponding amino acid carboxylate.
reactions.reasoning: The final hydrolysis from the alpha-aminonitrile precursor to arginine is the closing step implied in the cyanosulfidic route. Quantitative continuous demonstration from simplest feedstocks remains unresolved, so this step should be viewed as plausible completion rather than fully solved end-to-end synthesis (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Direct aqueous guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration; basic aqueous conditions conceptually related to ornithine guanylation
conditions.other: historic guanylation demonstrated under basic conditions; modern aqueous guanylation by cyanamide reaches high yield with non-prebiotic Sc(OTf)3, which is not invoked here

reactions.mechanism: The delta-amino group of ornithine attacks cyanamide, directly installing the guanidinium precursor to form arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic two-phase arginine proposal: first obtain ornithine, then guanylate with cyanamide. The core reaction is historically established by Schulze & Winterstein and chemically validated in water by later studies, but not demonstrated as a continuous fully prebiotic route; therefore it is included as literature-supported but incomplete (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Provision of ornithine from spark-discharge/surface prebiotic amino acid inventory

conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge/UV-driven atmospheric synthesis followed by surface concentration
conditions.other: represents literature-detected ornithine occurrence in volcanic spark discharge products rather than a single resolved mechanistic step

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge or complex HCN-derived prebiotic synthesis yields trace ornithine, which is then concentrated in surface environments.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports ornithine as prebiotically available because it was detected in Miller volcanic spark-discharge reanalyses and tentatively in Murchison, but does not provide a resolved simple mechanistic route from primitive feedstocks. This node is therefore explicitly a provisioning/concentration reaction, included only because the ornithine branch cannot otherwise be connected without violating the literature (Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Meierhenrich et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Provision of cyanate from HCN oligomer hydrolysis chemistry
reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis generate reactive C-N-O species including cyanate within a broader nitrile hydrolysate pool.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanate is literature-discussed as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl equivalent and HCN hydrolysates provide related products, but detailed stoichiometry is not established in the provided report. This step is therefore marked as a cautious provisioning node rather than a precise mechanistic claim (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Hypothetical carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: anoxic; prebiotic urea-cycle analog, explicitly speculative

reactions.mechanism: Carbamoylation of the terminal amino group of ornithine yields citrulline using cyanate as a stable carbamoyl source.
reactions.reasoning: This branch follows the literature-proposed prebiotic urea-cycle analog. Citrulline has been identified in HCN oligomer hydrolysates, and cyanate is proposed as a more kinetically stable carbamoyl donor than carbamoyl phosphate, but no direct prebiotic ornithine-to-citrulline experiment under realistic conditions is established; therefore this node is explicitly speculative (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Speculative conversion of citrulline plus cyanamide to arginine

conditions.other: prebiotic pool or mineral interface; explicitly hypothetical

reactions.mechanism: Net guanidino-group elaboration from citrulline under cyanamide-rich conditions is proposed as a non-enzymatic analog of later arginine biosynthesis.
reactions.reasoning: No experimental prebiotic conversion of citrulline to arginine is established in the supplied literature. This reaction is retained only because the deep research explicitly identifies a hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog as one proposed route. It should be treated as exploratory and low-confidence (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Cunin et al., 1986).

reactions.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O arginine formation from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine

conditions.catalyst: supercritical CO2/H2O medium; mineral agents used here as hydrothermal surfaces, not as demonstrated essential catalysts
conditions.other: 60C supercritical CO2/H2O after acid hydrolysis; trace yield only

reactions.mechanism: Poorly resolved multicomponent hydrothermal synthesis gives trace arginine among amino acid products after hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is included only as a marginal literature edge case. Fujioka et al. reported 0.32 micromol arginine from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions after acid hydrolysis, but the mechanism was not elucidated and the route is weak relative to arginine lability at elevated temperatures. It should not be interpreted as a robust pathway (Fujioka et al., 2009; Miller & Bada, 1988; Korner et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-mediated concentration and protection of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7 interlayer adsorption at clay-water interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Cation exchange places guanidinium-containing arginine into clay interlayers, increasing local concentration and reducing degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Although not a net synthetic transformation, this is chemically important to preserve and concentrate any arginine once formed. Montmorillonite selectively adsorbs arginine 30-50x more strongly than zwitterionic amino acids, making it a realistic post-synthetic stabilization process (Catalano et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface production/provision of serine from prebiotic amino acid inventory
reactions.mechanism: Representative supply of a simpler amino acid into surface pools for downstream peptide chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Serine is included only to allow a valid downstream peptide-forming biochemical branch once arginine exists. Simpler amino acids are broadly supported in prebiotic synthesis experiments and meteorites, unlike arginine (Miller, 1953; Higgs & Pudritz, 2009).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide bond formation incorporating arginine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces; deliquescent salt or clay-assisted concentration

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation during wet-dry cycles forms peptide bonds from concentrated amino acids, including clay-associated arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is a downstream utilization branch rather than evidence for arginine origin, but it closes the Surface-to-Biochemical transition realistically. Wet-dry cycling and mineral mediation enhance peptide formation, and arginine in montmorillonite interlayers can participate in high-temperature condensation contexts (Campbell et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998).

pathways.name: Core cyanosulfidic aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Best-supported constructive route from HCN and acetylene through beta-aminopropionaldehyde and cyanamide to an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor, then hydrolysis to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with hydrothermal reduced-carbon backdrop
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate/acetate provisioning feeds a reduced-carbon environment that interfaces with the surface cyanosulfidic branch to arginine.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation route
pathways.description: Two-phase strategy in which prebiotically provisioned ornithine reacts directly with cyanamide to form arginine.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation with montmorillonite protection
pathways.description: Arginine formed by ornithine guanylation is immediately concentrated/protected on clay surfaces.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route followed by clay protection
pathways.description: The core Patel/Sutherland branch produces arginine, which is then captured in montmorillonite interlayers.

pathways.name: Hypothetical cyanate-citrulline prebiotic urea-cycle analog
pathways.description: Exploratory branch in which HCN-derived cyanate carbamoylates ornithine to citrulline, followed by speculative conversion to arginine.

pathways.name: Citrulline branch aided by independent cyanamide pool
pathways.description: Speculative urea-cycle analog strengthened by a separate cyanamide source before the putative citrulline-to-arginine conversion.

pathways.name: Trace supercritical pyruvate-hydroxylamine route
pathways.description: Marginal literature-reported trace arginine formation from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route to arginine followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine formed through the best-supported precursor route is incorporated into a simple dipeptide under wet-dry biochemical conditions.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation to peptide-incorporated arginine
pathways.description: Ornithine plus cyanamide yields arginine, which then enters downstream peptide chemistry in concentrated prebiotic pools.
